Detection Boundaries

What ESA detects. And what it doesn't.

No condition monitoring technology detects everything. Vibration misses submerged assets. Thermography misses internal faults. ESA has boundaries too. This page documents every one: the physics, the measurement constraints, the algorithmic requirements, and what Samotics does to work within them.

Structured using a MECE framework: Electrical Preconditions, Mechanical and Operational Preconditions, Signal Quality Constraints, Installation and Measurement Constraints, and Algorithmic and Detection Constraints.

Talk to an Engineer See what we do detect →
1. Electrical Preconditions

The electrical environment ESA requires

ESA treats the motor as a transducer. The electrical supply must be clean enough, balanced enough, and isolated enough for fault signatures to be distinguishable from supply artifacts.

Three-phase AC induction motors

ESA diagnostic models are built around polyphase rotating fields in AC induction machines. The fault libraries, sideband calculations, and Park/Clarke vector features all assume three-phase symmetry. Single-phase and DC motors are not supported. Synchronous motors are supported with adapted fault models.

What Samotics does: Covers >95% of industrial motors above 5 kW. Synchronous motor support on request.

Three-phase voltage and current measurement

Full ESA requires 3-phase current and 3-phase voltage (3I+3V). Current alone (MCSA) supports a reduced fault set. Voltage enables power quality separation, negative-sequence analysis, and power signature features. Without voltage, supply-side artifacts can be misclassified as mechanical faults.

What Samotics does: SAM4 measures 3I+3V as standard. Power quality is a separate detection class, not a confounding variable.

Supply voltage unbalance below 2%

Voltage unbalance creates a negative-sequence current component that mimics or masks fault signatures. A 1% voltage unbalance can produce 6 to 10% current unbalance. Above 2%, eccentricity and stator diagnostics become unreliable. The NEMA/NREL recommendation is below 1% at motor terminals.

What Samotics does: SAM4 measures voltage unbalance continuously and classifies power quality issues separately from asset health conditions.

Supply voltage THD below 5%

Harmonic distortion from the grid, rectifiers, or neighbouring equipment creates spectral content that competes with fault signatures. IEEE 519 recommends below 5% THD-V at the point of common coupling for low-voltage systems. Above this, the background spectrum becomes dense enough to mask diagnostic sidebands.

What Samotics does: SAM4 tracks harmonic distortion as a power quality feature. When THD is elevated, the system adjusts confidence thresholds and flags the environment.

One motor per measurement channel

ESA requires current measurement on an individual motor feeder. Shared feeders or parallel motors on one breaker create crosstalk: fault signatures from one motor appear in signals from neighbouring motors. Without per-motor isolation, fault attribution collapses. This is a knock-out criterion.

What Samotics does: Per-motor feeder access is mandatory. Verified during the coverage assessment before deployment.
2. Mechanical and Operational Preconditions

The operating conditions ESA needs

These boundaries govern whether fault signatures are physically strong enough to detect. They depend on load, speed, slip, duty cycle, and drivetrain topology.

Slip and load: the real detection driver

The primary variable for rotor fault detection is slip, not load percentage. Rotor bar sidebands sit at f1(1 ± 2s). When slip is small, sidebands compress toward the fundamental and become unresolvable. Load is a proxy for slip, but high-efficiency motors (IE3/IE4) have low full-load slip, making rotor bar detection harder even at 100% load. As a practical guideline: below 30% of rated load, classical spectral methods lose sensitivity for most fault types.

What Samotics does: SAM4 estimates slip per motor and per operating point. Low-load and low-slip periods are flagged and excluded from diagnostic windows. Wavelet methods extend rotor fault detection to lower load ranges.

Speed, pole count, and frequency resolution

The constraint is not RPM itself but the spacing between fault-related sidebands and the fundamental frequency. Low-speed, high-pole-count motors produce tightly spaced sidebands that require longer analysis windows (64 to 128 seconds at very low slip) and higher frequency resolution. Below approximately 300 RPM, or with VFD output below 20 to 30 Hz, classical FFT methods become fragile without frequency tracking or demodulation.

What Samotics does: Custom signal processing for low-speed assets. Adaptive analysis window length based on estimated slip. Detection at 80% for Archimedes screws, improving with each release.

Steady-state window: 45 to 120 seconds

High-resolution spectral analysis requires quasi-constant speed and load for the duration of the analysis window. The minimum depends on slip: low-slip motors need longer windows. If speed or load changes materially within the window, classical FFT produces smeared spectra. Assets that cycle rapidly or never reach steady state require time-frequency methods.

What Samotics does: SAM4 classifies operating points automatically and selects stable windows. Time-frequency methods (wavelet, order tracking) extend coverage to variable-duty assets.

Duty cycle and run-time adequacy

Assets that run briefly, rarely, or at a single narrow operating point yield limited data for baseline construction and trending. Practical thresholds: at least 5 minutes of continuous operation per diagnostic snapshot, and at least 20 operating cycles across each load/speed regime for a robust baseline. Intermittent assets (crushers, batch pumps) take longer to reach full detection confidence.

What Samotics does: SAM4 tracks operating point coverage and reports when baseline data is insufficient. Confidence levels are visible per asset in the dashboard.

Drivetrain complexity and transfer path

Mechanical faults must modulate motor torque to appear in the current signal. The torque transfer path determines how much fault energy reaches the motor. Direct-coupled loads produce the strongest signatures. Gearboxes attenuate and reshape fault energy. Multi-stage transmissions, compliant couplings, and long shafts reduce detection confidence. The constraint is transfer-path simplicity, not physical distance.

What Samotics does: SAM4 maps each drivetrain topology at installation. Detection expectations are set per configuration: direct-coupled, single-stage gearbox, or multi-stage.
3. Signal Quality Constraints

The signal integrity ESA demands

Even when the motor, load, and electrical supply meet all preconditions, the acquired signal must have sufficient dynamic range, frequency resolution, and freedom from interference to support diagnosis.

VFD switching noise and control artifacts

Variable frequency drives introduce PWM harmonics, interharmonics, and control-loop artifacts that mask diagnostic sidebands. At low output frequencies (below 20 to 30 Hz), fault-related components compress toward the fundamental and VFD artifacts dominate. Slip-compensation control modes can suppress the rotor bar signatures that classical MCSA relies on. Detection confidence depends on carrier frequency, control mode, and measurement location (line-side vs. motor-side vs. embedded).

What Samotics does: SAM4 applies adaptive harmonic isolation per VFD topology. For VFD-fed assets, performance is validated per installation during commissioning.

Measurement chain dynamic range

Fault signatures in motor current are typically 40 to 60 dB below the fundamental component. The CT, signal conditioning, and ADC must resolve these small nonfundamental components against the dominant fundamental. A minimum dynamic range of approximately 80 dB is required. If the measurement chain cannot deliver this, fault signatures disappear into the noise floor regardless of motor size or load.

What Samotics does: SAM4 hardware is specified for >80 dB dynamic range across the diagnostic frequency band.

Environmental electromagnetic interference

Adjacent VFDs, welding equipment, large power converters, and other EMI sources raise the noise floor in the measurement cabinet. When environmental noise correlates with fault frequency bands, false positives increase. When it raises the broadband floor, small fault signatures become undetectable.

What Samotics does: SAM4 installation guidelines specify cable routing and shielding requirements. Operating point gating excludes periods of elevated environmental noise.
4. Installation and Measurement Constraints

The hardware and access ESA requires

These constraints define the physical installation: where to measure, what sensors to use, and what metadata is needed for accurate fault mapping.

Motor-specific measurement access

Current transformers must be installed on an individual motor feeder. The most common location is the motor control cabinet (MCC). Alternatives include VFD-embedded measurement, switchgear-level access, or clamp-on CTs at the motor cable. For VFD-fed motors, motor-side or embedded measurement is preferred. Line-side measurement requires explicit harmonic isolation modelling. Physical access is mandatory during installation only.

What Samotics does: After initial installation, all monitoring is remote. No ongoing site visits required.

CT accuracy class and frequency response

Protection-grade CTs (e.g. Class 10P10) lack the accuracy and harmonic fidelity required for ESA. Measurement-grade CTs with accuracy class 0.5S or better are required. Operating current must be above 5 to 10% of the CT's rated primary current; below this, accuracy is not guaranteed. CT frequency response must support the diagnostic band: minimum WB1 (3 kHz), preferred WB2 (20 kHz) for bearing and high-frequency content.

What Samotics does: SAM4 uses measurement-grade CTs specified per installation. CT ratio, class, and frequency response are part of deployment engineering.

Nameplate metadata: pole count, rotor bars, bearings

Accurate fault frequency calculation requires motor nameplate data. Rotor bar fault sidebands depend on pole count and slip. Bearing fault frequencies depend on bearing geometry (BPFO, BPFI, BSF, FTF). Without accurate metadata, the system cannot place expected fault frequencies and must rely on anomaly detection alone, reducing diagnostic specificity.

What Samotics does: SAM4 onboarding captures nameplate data per asset. Where bearing geometry is unknown, SAM4 uses manufacturer databases or generic models with reduced specificity.
5. Algorithmic and Detection Constraints

What the software needs to know

These constraints are not physics limitations. They are requirements of the detection algorithms: what data they need, what assumptions they make, and where they have known blind spots.

Baseline: operating envelope coverage

SAM4 needs baseline data covering the machine's normal operating points before full detection capability. Duration depends on how quickly the asset cycles through its load and speed range. Continuous-duty assets on fixed load baseline in hours. Variable-duty assets with multiple speed/load regimes require days to weeks. The baseline must span the machine's real operating envelope, not just a single state.

What Samotics does: Most deployments reach full detection within 48 hours of sufficient runtime across operating points. SAM4 reports operating point coverage in the dashboard.

Fault-type-specific detection sensitivity

ESA does not have a single detection threshold. Rotor bar faults, eccentricity, stator faults, bearing faults, hydraulic disturbances, and drivetrain faults each have different observability limits. Rotor faults need sufficient slip. Bearing faults need sufficient load and frequency response. Mechanical faults need a direct torque coupling. Detection confidence is always fault-class-dependent and operating-point-dependent.

What Samotics does: SAM4 reports detection confidence per fault type per asset. The asset-specific detection table below reflects these differences.

Non-adjacent broken bar blind spot

When two broken rotor bars are separated by exactly half a pole pitch (90 degrees electrical), their fault signatures can cancel in the stator current. This is the most documented false negative in MCSA literature. The motor appears healthy in the current spectrum despite having multiple broken bars. This applies to any ESA or MCSA system, not only SAM4.

What Samotics does: SAM4 uses multiple diagnostic features beyond classical sideband amplitude (including power signature and negative-sequence analysis) to reduce this blind spot. It is not fully eliminated.

Operating regime must match algorithm

Steady-state FFT works for line-fed motors at constant load. VFD-fed motors with varying speed require order tracking or time-frequency methods. Transient machines need wavelet or AR-based preprocessing. If the algorithm family does not match the operating regime, the system produces either missed faults or false alarms.

What Samotics does: SAM4 selects the analysis method per operating regime automatically. Operating point classification drives algorithm selection, not a one-size-fits-all approach.
Asset-Specific Detection Rates

Detection varies by asset type and fault class

The five constraint categories above set the physics and engineering. Detection accuracy also depends on the asset being monitored and the specific fault type. Some faults produce stronger electrical signatures than others.

Asset Type Overall Detection Strongest Fault Known Limitation
Pumps 84.6% Cavitation, impeller wear Motor bearing: 30.8%. No direct seal leak detection
LV Motors 67.4% Misalignment, unbalance Small motors (<5 kW): limited SNR
MV Motors Strong Insulation, rotor bar Partial discharge: indicative only, not diagnostic
Fans & Blowers Good Unbalance, bearing (driven) Motor bearing: 50%. Blade defects: 66.7%
Conveyors Good Belt misalignment, bearing Belt tension below detection threshold
Compressors 37.5% Load profile changes Variable-load profiles limit steady-state windows
Transmissions Good Gear mesh, coupling Coupling: 57.1%. Cannot isolate specific gear stage
Archimedes Screws 80% Bearing, unbalance Low RPM requires extended analysis windows. Trough wear below threshold
Canned Motor Pumps Early stage Thermal, flow anomalies No verified detection data yet. Sealed can attenuates torque path
ESPs Developing Flow, electrical faults Cable length and VSD harmonics affect accuracy

For full detection boundaries per asset type, see the individual monitoring pages.

Explicit Non-Detections

Faults ESA cannot detect

These fault types are outside ESA's detection capability, or require complementary technologies for reliable diagnosis.

Mechanical seal leaks

ESA does not detect seal leaks directly. The root causes that accelerate seal failure (misalignment, unbalance, cavitation) are among SAM4's strongest detection categories.

Partial discharge (diagnostic)

ESA provides a screening indicator for partial discharge on MV motors. It cannot replace dedicated PD testing. Use ESA for early warning, then confirm with offline insulation testing.

Power quality upstream of VFDs

SAM4 detects faults between the drive and the motor. Power quality issues upstream of the VFD are not visible in the motor current signal.

Process conditions without torque impact

Pipe blockages, fluid temperature changes, and chemical composition shifts do not always produce detectable electrical signatures. Where they affect pump load, ESA detects the load change but cannot diagnose the process cause.

Structural faults without torque coupling

A mechanical fault that does not create a cyclic torque variation on the motor shaft is invisible to ESA. Examples: structural resonance in a fan housing, foundation looseness that does not load the bearings cyclically, pipe-borne vibration that bypasses the drivetrain.

Incipient bearing defects (Stage 1-2)

In the earliest stages of bearing failure, defect energy is primarily ultrasonic and does not modulate motor current detectably. ESA identifies bearing issues from Stage 3 onward. For early-stage detection on accessible, high-value assets, vibration analysis is more sensitive.

Dual-fault masking

Two faults producing signatures at the same frequency can interfere destructively, causing underestimation of severity. A common case: belt-pass frequency coinciding with rotor bar sideband spacing. SAM4's multi-feature approach reduces this risk but does not eliminate it.

Non-adjacent broken bars (half pole-pitch separation)

Broken rotor bars separated by exactly half a pole pitch produce cancelling fault components in the stator current. The motor appears healthy in the spectrum. This blind spot is inherent to all current-based rotor bar diagnosis. SAM4 mitigates it with multi-feature analysis but cannot fully eliminate it.

Pushing the Boundaries

Where SAM4 extends beyond classical limits

Classical MCSA literature defines conservative boundaries. Samotics actively pushes them. Four areas where SAM4 operates beyond standard textbook limits.

Low-load rotor fault detection

Classical FFT methods lose sensitivity below 30% load because sidebands merge with the fundamental's spectral leakage. SAM4 uses wavelet-based decomposition to isolate rotor fault signatures at lower load ranges by separating fault energy from the fundamental frequency component.

VFD harmonic isolation

Standard spectral analysis fails with VFD-fed motors because PWM harmonics and control artifacts dominate the spectrum. SAM4 applies adaptive harmonic isolation (including negative-sequence and Kalman filter methods) to subtract VFD carrier harmonics. This restores bearing and rotor bar detection in inverter environments.

Transient startup analysis

During motor startup, the rotor passes through high slip values. Fault sidebands sweep across the frequency spectrum, making rotor bar defects visible in a 5 to 10 second window. SAM4 captures and analyses this transient, providing diagnostic information even when steady-state windows are unavailable.

Low-speed frequency tracking

At VFD output frequencies below 20 to 30 Hz, classical FFT-based MCSA is fragile. SAM4 uses frequency tracking and demodulation methods designed for low-frequency, low-slip conditions. This extends rotor and mechanical fault detection to assets that classical methods cannot cover.

Complementary, Not Competitive

Where ESA leads. Where vibration leads.

ESA and vibration are complementary technologies. Each has environments where it excels and environments where it struggles.

ESA leads when:

  • Assets are submerged, enclosed, or in hazardous zones
  • Physical access to the machine is impossible or expensive
  • Fleet-wide monitoring from a single MCC is needed
  • Electrical faults (insulation, phase imbalance, rotor bar) matter most
  • Continuous 24/7 monitoring without site visits is required
  • Power quality issues need to be separated from mechanical faults

Vibration leads when:

  • Early-stage bearing defect classification (Stage 1-2) is critical
  • The asset is directly accessible and sensor mounting is practical
  • Structural resonance or foundation looseness needs diagnosis
  • Component-level isolation in multi-stage gearboxes is required
  • Single high-value asset justifies dedicated instrumentation
  • Historical vibration baselines already exist
Most Samotics customers deploy ESA alongside existing vibration programmes. ESA covers the assets vibration cannot reach. Vibration provides component-level precision on accessible assets. Together, they close the monitoring gap.

Understand what SAM4 can detect in your plant.

Every deployment starts with a coverage assessment. Our engineers map your assets against these boundary conditions, identify which ones ESA can monitor, and set clear expectations on detection capability per fault type.
